docs.title: Prostate Cancer Research Library
docs.text:
This is a curated, patient-perspective research interface for prostate cancer diagnosis and treatment decisions.

Not medical advice

This repository summarizes research for education and clinician discussion. It does not diagnose, prescribe, recommend a treatment, or replace medical care.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The repository is designed to help a potential patient understand:

  • What tests and diagnostic steps usually mean
  • How clinicians classify risk and stage disease
  • Treatment options and trade-offs
  • Side effects and quality-of-life outcomes
  • When active surveillance may be considered
  • How new evidence may change questions to ask clinicians

docs.title: Default evidence hierarchy
docs.text:
1. Current clinical guidelines and consensus statements from reputable oncology/urology bodies
  2. Randomized controlled trials and high-quality meta-analyses
  3. Large prospective cohorts and registry studies
  4. Diagnostic accuracy studies with clinically relevant outcomes
  5. Patient-reported outcome studies
  6. Mechanistic, preclinical, or hypothesis-generating studies, clearly labeled

docs.title: Core patient questions
docs.text:
• Is cancer present, and how certain is that conclusion?
  • If present, how aggressive does it appear to be?
  • What are the reasonable management options for this risk group?
  • What are the likely benefits, harms, uncertainties, and quality-of-life effects?
  • What would change the decision later?

docs.title: Category Index
docs.text:
Use these categories to file studies, guideline updates, patient decision aids, and clinical questions.

Category What belongs here Patient-facing question Screening & Early Detection PSA, MRI-first pathways, risk calculators, screening intervals, overdiagnosis Should I be tested, retested, or monitored? Risk Stratification & Staging Gleason/Grade Group, PSA density, TNM, nomograms, genomic classifiers How aggressive is it? Active Surveillance Eligibility, monitoring schedules, triggers for treatment, long-term outcomes Can treatment safely wait? Localized Treatment Surgery, radiation modalities, focal therapy, comparative outcomes What are the curative options and trade-offs? Advanced & Recurrent Disease ADT, AR pathway inhibitors, chemotherapy, radioligand therapy, recurrence What if it has spread or returns? Imaging & Biomarkers mpMRI, PSMA PET, liquid/tissue biomarkers, genomic tests What extra tests might clarify risk? Side Effects & Quality of Life Urinary, sexual, bowel, fatigue, mental health, survivorship What will life be like after treatment? Nutrition Diet quality, body composition, metabolic health, supplements, nutrition during treatment and survivorship What nutrition evidence is relevant, and what should I discuss with a clinician or dietitian? Exercise Resistance training, aerobic fitness, fatigue, function, bone/muscle health, exercise during treatment and survivorship What activity is safe and useful in my situation? Shared Decision-Making Decision aids, patient preferences, communication, second opinions How do I choose between reasonable options? docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Research notes and filed articles about exercise, physical function, resistance training, aerobic fitness, fatigue, treatment tolerance, and quality of life in prostate cancer diagnosis, treatment, and survivorship.

This category is for evidence that helps patients discuss safe, appropriate activity plans with clinicians and exercise professionals.

docs.title: Key patient questions
docs.text:
• What kinds of exercise improve fatigue, function, strength, mood, or quality of life during or after prostate cancer treatment?
  • What is known about exercise during active surveillance, after prostatectomy, during radiation, or during ADT?
  • Can resistance training help reduce muscle and bone loss associated with ADT?
  • What safety limits apply after surgery, during radiation, or with bone metastases?
  • What should be discussed with a clinician, physiotherapist, or accredited exercise physiologist?

docs.title: Evidence to prioritize
docs.text:
• Randomized trials and meta-analyses of exercise interventions in prostate cancer populations
  • Studies measuring fatigue, strength, cardiorespiratory fitness, falls, bone density, sexual/urinary function, mood, and quality of life
  • Exercise oncology guidelines and consensus statements
  • Safety studies for patients on ADT or with advanced/metastatic disease

docs.title: Cautions
docs.text:
Not medical advice

Exercise notes are educational. People undergoing diagnosis or treatment should discuss exercise plans with a qualified clinician, especially after surgery, during systemic therapy, or if bone metastases, severe fatigue, anemia, cardiovascular disease, falls risk, or pain are present.

Be especially cautious with:

  • Heavy lifting soon after surgery
  • High-impact exercise with bone metastases or fracture risk
  • Intense exercise during acute illness, severe fatigue, or treatment complications
  • Generic fitness advice not studied in prostate cancer populations

docs.title: Filed items
docs.text: • Exercise and Prostate Cancer: Evidence Map — curated evidence summary covering exercise/physical activity before diagnosis, after diagnosis, during treatment, and after treatment/survivorship. Includes incidence evidence, post-diagnosis survival/progression cohorts, radiation/ADT trials, pelvic-floor rehabilitation evidence, and exercise-oncology guidance.

docs.title: Current evidence summary
docs.text: Patient phase Summary Before diagnosis / incidence Exercise is highly beneficial for general health, but prostate-cancer-specific incidence prevention evidence is mixed. Best meta-analyses show little or no association with total prostate cancer incidence, with possible but uncertain reductions in advanced/aggressive/fatal disease. After diagnosis Higher post-diagnosis activity, especially brisk walking and vigorous activity, is consistently associated with better survival/progression outcomes in observational cohorts, but causality is not proven. During treatment Randomized trials and systematic reviews support aerobic and resistance exercise during radiation and ADT for fatigue, fitness, strength, function, body composition, and quality of life. After treatment Survivorship exercise supports function and quality of life. Pelvic-floor muscle exercise, especially supervised programs, has specific evidence for urinary incontinence after radical prostatectomy.

docs.title: Practical patient resources
docs.text: • Five exercises to strengthen your pelvic floor — UT Health San Antonio MD Anderson Cancer Center patient resource. Useful as a practical discussion aid for pelvic-floor strengthening, especially around urinary-control recovery, but it should be checked with the treating surgeon, urologist, physiotherapist, or pelvic-floor clinician before use after prostate surgery or during active treatment.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Research notes and filed articles about nutrition, diet quality, body composition, metabolic health, and prostate cancer diagnosis/treatment outcomes.

This category is for patient-facing evidence on nutrition as a supportive health factor, not as a substitute for clinically indicated cancer diagnosis or treatment.

docs.title: Key patient questions
docs.text:
• What dietary patterns are associated with prostate cancer risk, progression, recurrence, or treatment tolerance?
  • What is known about body weight, body composition, insulin resistance, cardiometabolic health, and outcomes?
  • Are there nutrition considerations during active surveillance, surgery, radiation, ADT, chemotherapy, or survivorship?
  • Which claims are supported by human clinical evidence, and which remain speculative?
  • What should be discussed with an oncologist, urologist, dietitian, or GP?

docs.title: Evidence to prioritize
docs.text:
• Human randomized trials and high-quality meta-analyses
  • Large prospective cohort studies with clinically meaningful endpoints
  • Studies of diet quality, Mediterranean-style patterns, protein adequacy, weight change, metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and cardiovascular risk in prostate cancer populations
  • Patient-reported outcomes and treatment-tolerance studies
  • Nutrition guidance from reputable cancer organizations

docs.title: Cautions
docs.text:
Not medical advice

Nutrition notes are educational and should not be used to delay diagnosis, avoid treatment, or replace advice from a qualified clinician.

Be especially cautious with:

  • High-dose supplements or concentrated extracts
  • Claims that a diet can cure cancer
  • Major weight-loss or fasting programs during active treatment
  • Interactions between supplements and surgery, radiation, ADT, chemotherapy, anticoagulants, or other medications

docs.title: Filed items
docs.text: • Nutrition and Prostate Cancer: Evidence Map — curated evidence summary covering diet and dietary patterns before diagnosis, after diagnosis, during treatment/ADT, and after treatment/survivorship. Includes incidence evidence, active surveillance studies and trials, ADT/metabolic-support interventions, and survivorship nutrition reviews.

docs.title: Current evidence summary
docs.text: Patient phase Summary Before diagnosis / incidence Overall dietary patterns matter more than single-food claims. Western/inflammatory patterns and high dairy/calcium intake are associated with higher risk in some observational syntheses; healthy/plant-forward patterns may be associated with lower risk, but causality is uncertain. After diagnosis Post-diagnosis plant-based, Mediterranean, and lower-inflammatory dietary patterns are associated with lower progression risk in several cohorts, but this is mostly observational. During treatment / ADT Diet and weight-management interventions may support weight, glucose, insulin resistance, lipids, and function during ADT, but evidence is mainly supportive-care rather than cancer-control evidence. After treatment Survivorship nutrition is strongest as cardiometabolic, bowel, muscle, weight, and quality-of-life support; evidence for recurrence/survival benefit is promising but not definitive.

docs.title: Blood Pressure, Hypertension, and Prostate Cancer: Evidence Map
docs.text:
Retrieved: 2026-07-04 Categories: Side Effects & Quality of Life; Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Screening & Early Detection Scope: blood pressure, hypertension, antihypertensive medication, prostate cancer incidence/fatal risk, recurrence/progression, and treatment-related hypertension/cardiovascular risk.

Not medical advice

This page summarizes research for education and clinician discussion. It does not diagnose hypertension, recommend a blood pressure target, or advise starting/stopping blood pressure or prostate cancer medication. Blood pressure goals and medication decisions should be individualized with qualified clinicians, especially in people with frailty, dizziness/falls risk, kidney disease, heart disease, diabetes, or prostate cancer therapies that can raise blood pressure.

docs.title: Patient-facing bottom line
docs.text: Question Best-supported conclusion Strength of evidence Practical clinician question Does high blood pressure cause prostate cancer? Evidence is mixed and not definitive. Hypertension is part of broader cardiometabolic risk; some cohorts/meta-analyses suggest associations with prostate cancer risk or fatal disease, but causality is uncertain. Low-to-moderate observational evidence Does my blood pressure affect overall risk management even if the prostate-cancer-specific link is uncertain? Does blood pressure matter after diagnosis? Hypertension may affect recurrence or survival indirectly through cardiovascular health and treatment tolerance. A 2026 solid-cancer recurrence meta-analysis did not show a clear overall recurrence association, but prostate-specific evidence remains limited. Low-to-moderate, mostly observational How should blood pressure and cardiovascular risk be monitored during surveillance and survivorship? Can prostate cancer treatments raise blood pressure? Yes. ADT and androgen-receptor pathway inhibitors can worsen cardiometabolic risk; abiraterone and enzalutamide/apalutamide-class agents are associated with increased hypertension risk in meta-analyses. Moderate-to-high for treatment-related hypertension signal Before starting ADT/AR pathway therapy, should my BP, lipids, glucose/HbA1c, kidney function, and cardiovascular history be reviewed? Does controlling blood pressure improve prostate cancer outcomes? Direct proof that BP treatment improves prostate cancer control is limited. The strongest reason to control BP is prevention of stroke, heart failure, kidney disease, and cardiovascular events, which are major competing risks in prostate cancer patients. High for cardiovascular benefit; limited for cancer-control benefit What BP target is appropriate for me, balancing cardiovascular prevention with dizziness/falls risk?

docs.title: 1. Before diagnosis: incidence and fatal prostate cancer risk
docs.text:
The literature does not support a simple statement that hypertension definitely causes prostate cancer. Metabolic syndrome meta-analyses suggest the broader cardiometabolic cluster may be associated with prostate cancer risk or aggressive features, but individual components vary and confounding is substantial.

The ARIC cohort adds a more specific signal: untreated stage 1 hypertension was associated with higher fatal prostate cancer risk, particularly in Black men, while antihypertensive use was associated with lower fatal prostate cancer risk. This is important but observational; it may reflect both biology and healthcare access/treatment differences.

Interpretation: high blood pressure should be treated seriously for cardiovascular health. Its independent role in prostate cancer incidence remains uncertain, but it belongs in the patient’s risk and resilience discussion.

docs.title: 2. After diagnosis: recurrence, progression, and survivorship
docs.text:
Direct prostate-specific evidence linking hypertension to recurrence/progression is limited. A 2026 systematic review across adult solid-cancer survivors found no clear overall association between hypertension and recurrence, but cancer types and treatments varied, and prostate-specific certainty remains low.

In practice, the main postdiagnosis relevance is that blood pressure is a modifiable competing-risk factor: men with prostate cancer may live many years, and cardiovascular disease can be a major source of morbidity and mortality. Blood pressure also affects eligibility, monitoring, and safety for some systemic therapies.

docs.title: 3. During treatment: ADT and androgen-receptor pathway therapy
docs.text:
This is the strongest clinical link. ADT is associated with adverse metabolic changes, including metabolic syndrome and diabetes risk, and may worsen cardiovascular risk. Novel androgen-receptor pathway inhibitors can also raise cardiovascular risk.

Specific treatment signals:

  • Abiraterone can cause mineralocorticoid-related toxicity, including hypertension, hypokalemia, and fluid retention; meta-analysis shows increased hypertension risk.
  • Enzalutamide is associated with increased hypertension risk in randomized-trial meta-analysis.
  • Apalutamide/enzalutamide/darolutamide class in nmCRPC: meta-analysis found increased cardiovascular events and grade 3–4 hypertension for novel hormonal agents plus ADT versus ADT/placebo.
  • Broader AR pathway inhibitor meta-analyses show increased cardiovascular events, including hypertension and heart failure signals in some analyses.

Interpretation: BP should be assessed and optimized before and during ADT/AR pathway treatment. This is a treatment-safety issue, not just a general health issue.

docs.title: 4. After treatment has ended: long-term survivorship
docs.text: After local treatment or systemic therapy, blood pressure remains relevant because cardiovascular disease is common and modifiable. Survivorship care should include BP measurement, lipid/glucose assessment, weight/body composition, physical activity, diet quality, medication review, and coordination between oncology/urology, primary care, and cardiology/cardio-oncology when risk is high.

docs.title: The Intersection of Prostate Cancer and Hypertension: a Call to Action
docs.text:
• Citation: Current Treatment Options in Oncology. 2023.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1007/s11864-023-01094-z; PMID 37191906
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37191906/
  • Date: 2023 Jul
  • Category: Side Effects & Quality of Life; Advanced & Recurrent Disease
  • Evidence type: Narrative review / clinical call to action.
  • Population: Men with prostate cancer, especially those exposed to ADT, GnRH agonists/antagonists, enzalutamide, abiraterone, and related therapies.
  • Patient relevance: Best high-level clinical overview of why BP matters in prostate cancer care.
  • Main finding: Prostate cancer therapies can directly or indirectly increase hypertension risk; individualized BP goals and proactive cardiovascular risk management are recommended.
  • Limitations: Review article, not a randomized trial; treatment decisions remain individualized.
  • Questions for clinician: Should BP be checked before treatment starts, and how often during ADT or androgen-receptor pathway therapy?

docs.title: Blood Pressure, Hypertension, and Antihypertensive Medication Use and Risk of Total and Fatal Prostate Cancer in ARIC
docs.text:
• Citation: Journal of Racial and Ethnic Health Disparities. 2025.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1007/s40615-023-01885-y; PMID 38099995
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38099995/
  • Date: 2025 Feb
  • Category: Screening & Early Detection; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Prospective cohort analysis.
  • Population: 6,658 men in the Atherosclerosis Risk in Communities study; 889 total and 127 fatal prostate cancer cases.
  • Patient relevance: Directly addresses BP, untreated hypertension, antihypertensive use, total prostate cancer, and fatal prostate cancer.
  • Main finding: Untreated stage 1 hypertension was associated with higher fatal prostate cancer risk compared with untreated normal BP; the association was stronger in Black men. Antihypertensive use was associated with lower fatal prostate cancer risk compared with never use.
  • Limitations: Observational; potential confounding by healthcare access, screening, treatment, adherence, and comorbidity.
  • Questions for clinician: Does my BP status and cardiovascular care need review as part of overall prostate cancer risk management?

docs.title: Effect of metabolic syndrome and its components on prostate cancer risk: meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Esposito K, et al. Journal of Endocrinological Investigation. 2013.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1007/BF03346748; PMID 23481613
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23481613/
  • Date: 2013 Feb
  • Category: Nutrition; Side Effects & Quality of Life; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Meta-analysis.
  • Population: 14 studies with 4,728 prostate cancer cases.
  • Patient relevance: Places hypertension in the broader metabolic syndrome/cardiometabolic context.
  • Main finding: Metabolic syndrome showed a modest, not statistically definitive overall association with prostate cancer risk; results differed by geography and study type.
  • Limitations: Older evidence base, heterogeneous definitions, observational confounding.
  • Questions for clinician: Should prostate cancer risk conversations include metabolic health, waist/weight, glucose, lipids, and BP together?

docs.title: Meta-analysis of metabolic syndrome and prostate cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: Prostate Cancer and Prostatic Diseases. 2017.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1038/pcan.2017.1; PMID 28220805
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28220805/
  • Date: 2017 Jun
  • Category: Nutrition; Side Effects & Quality of Life; Risk Stratification & Staging
  • Evidence type: Meta-analysis.
  • Population: Studies of metabolic syndrome, prostate cancer incidence, high-grade disease, aggressive pathology, and biochemical recurrence.
  • Patient relevance: Addresses whether cardiometabolic factors relate to more aggressive prostate cancer features.
  • Main finding: Metabolic syndrome and some components may relate to aggressive prostate cancer features, but associations vary by endpoint and component.
  • Limitations: Observational evidence; metabolic syndrome combines multiple risk factors, so hypertension-specific conclusions are limited.
  • Questions for clinician: Are BP, diabetes, lipids, and obesity being managed as part of overall cancer survivorship health?

docs.title: Hypertension and risk of cancer recurrence in adult survivors: systematic review and meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Hypertension Research. 2026.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1038/s41440-025-02431-5; PMID 41203853
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41203853/
  • Date: 2026 Mar
  • Category: Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies.
  • Population: 13 observational studies, 15 cohorts, 50,549 adult solid-cancer survivors.
  • Patient relevance: Addresses whether hypertension after cancer treatment is linked with recurrence risk.
  • Main finding: Overall, hypertension was not clearly associated with increased solid tumor recurrence risk, but evidence varied and prostate-specific certainty is limited.
  • Limitations: Multi-cancer evidence, observational studies, heterogeneous definitions and treatments.
  • Questions for clinician: Is BP being managed mainly for cardiovascular survivorship, while recognizing cancer-recurrence evidence is uncertain?

docs.title: Assessment and Management of Cardiovascular Risk Factors Among US Veterans With Prostate Cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: JAMA Network Open. 2021.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2021.0070; PMID 33625512
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33625512/
  • Date: 2021 Feb 1
  • Category: Side Effects & Quality of Life; Guidelines & Care Pathways
  • Evidence type: Cross-sectional health-system analysis.
  • Population: 90,494 US veterans with prostate cancer; includes men initiating ADT and men with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.
  • Patient relevance: Shows real-world gaps/opportunities in BP, cholesterol, and glucose assessment and management around prostate cancer care.
  • Main finding: Cardiovascular risk factor assessment and control are central survivorship issues, especially for men initiating ADT.
  • Limitations: Veterans Health Administration population; observational health-system data.
  • Questions for clinician: Have BP, LDL/cholesterol, glucose/HbA1c, smoking, weight, and exercise been explicitly reviewed before ADT?

docs.title: Treatment-related blood pressure and cardiovascular risk
Quantifying risk of metabolic syndrome and components following ADT: meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Bosco C, et al. PLOS ONE. 2015.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0117344; PMID 25794005
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25794005/
  • Date: 2015
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Meta-analysis.
  • Population: Nine studies of men receiving ADT.
  • Patient relevance: ADT is common in advanced/high-risk prostate cancer and can worsen metabolic risk.
  • Main finding: ADT was associated with increased metabolic syndrome risk; diabetes risk was also increased. Hypertension-specific evidence was less robust but part of the cardiometabolic risk frame.
  • Limitations: Limited number of studies for individual components; observational designs included.
  • Questions for clinician: What cardiometabolic monitoring schedule is needed when starting ADT?

docs.title: Pharmacologic androgen deprivation and cardiovascular disease risk factors: systematic review
docs.text:
• Citation: European Journal of Clinical Investigation. 2015.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1111/eci.12431; PMID 25753698
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25753698/
  • Date: 2015 May
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review of randomized controlled trials reporting cardiovascular risk factors.
  • Population: Men with prostate cancer in RCTs comparing pharmacologic ADT with no ADT.
  • Patient relevance: Looks at BP and other cardiovascular risk factors in a trial context.
  • Main finding: ADT affects cardiovascular risk factors; BP reporting was less consistent than other metabolic outcomes.
  • Limitations: Risk-factor outcomes were not always primary endpoints; trial populations may underrepresent frail patients or those with major comorbidities.
  • Questions for clinician: Which risk factors are most likely to worsen for me: BP, weight, glucose, lipids, or body composition?

docs.title: Association of ADT with metabolic disease in prostate cancer patients: updated meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Clinical Genitourinary Cancer. 2023.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.clgc.2022.12.006; PMID 36621463
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36621463/
  • Date: 2023 Jun
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Updated meta-analysis.
  • Population: 19 studies of ADT-associated metabolic outcomes.
  • Patient relevance: Quantifies metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and hypertension domains in ADT-treated men.
  • Main finding: ADT was associated with higher metabolic syndrome risk and diabetes risk; hypertension is part of the monitored metabolic disease burden.
  • Limitations: Heterogeneous study designs, controls, and definitions.
  • Questions for clinician: Should ADT care include baseline and follow-up BP, glucose/HbA1c, lipids, weight, and exercise/nutrition planning?

docs.title: Cardiovascular Events and Androgen Receptor Signaling Inhibitors in Advanced Prostate Cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: JAMA Oncology. 2024.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1001/jamaoncol.2024.1549; PMID 38842801
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38842801/
  • Date: 2024 Jul 1
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials.
  • Population: Men with locally advanced or metastatic prostate cancer treated with androgen receptor signaling inhibitors added to standard care.
  • Patient relevance: Directly relevant when considering abiraterone, enzalutamide, apalutamide, darolutamide-class intensification.
  • Main finding: AR signaling inhibitors improve prostate cancer outcomes in appropriate settings but add cardiovascular toxicity concerns, including hypertension-related monitoring needs.
  • Limitations: Trial populations may exclude high-risk cardiovascular patients; adverse event reporting varies.
  • Questions for clinician: What is the benefit of treatment intensification, and how will BP/cardiovascular toxicity be monitored and managed?

docs.title: Cardiovascular events among men treated with androgen receptor pathway inhibitors
docs.text:
• Citation: Prostate Cancer and Prostatic Diseases. 2025.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1038/s41391-024-00886-0; PMID 39237679
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39237679/
  • Date: 2025 Jun
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review, meta-analysis, and network meta-analysis of RCTs.
  • Population: 26 RCTs of abiraterone, apalutamide, darolutamide, and enzalutamide.
  • Patient relevance: Helps compare cardiovascular toxicity across AR pathway inhibitors.
  • Main finding: ARPIs were associated with increased cardiac disorders, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, and hypertension-related cardiovascular concern signals.
  • Limitations: Indirect comparisons, heterogeneous disease states and treatment durations.
  • Questions for clinician: If several ARPI options are reasonable, should cardiovascular history influence drug choice?
